summary: Gruber updates his challenge to security researchers David Maynor and Jon Ellch to demonstrate a Wi-Fi exploit on a stock MacBook, noting the bounty has doubled to two MacBooks. He methodically dismantles the claim that his challenge is unfair by tracing the contradictory and evasive statements Maynor and Ellch have made about whether the exploit affects built-in MacBook hardware. He argues their refusal to give a simple yes-or-no answer about the built-in card's vulnerability is itself suspicious. He also examines and largely debunks the theory that Apple Legal has silenced the researchers, noting the supposed threats don't appear in the published interview transcript.
key_points: Maynor and Ellch's own published Q&A contradicts itself — first implying the exploit affects the built-in MacBook card, then saying it doesn't, then vaguely referencing ongoing research.
The researchers have never simply stated they cannot perform the exploit on stock MacBook hardware, which would be the obvious response if the challenge were truly unfair.
Brian Krebs's interview transcript contains no mention of Apple pressuring the researchers, despite Krebs's original reporting claiming Apple 'leaned on' them.
Apple Legal gag-order speculation doesn't hold up: gag orders come from courts, apply to both sides, and Apple's own public statements suggest none exists.
The researchers' deliberate ambiguity about the exploit's scope is no more responsible than a clear confirmation would be.
money_quotes: If I lose — a big 'if' in my opinion — I'll set up a DropCash campaign for people to contribute to. But I issued this challenge fully willing to foot the entire bill, should I lose.
In fact, I'm pretty certain I could up the ante to a gold-plated MacBook at this point.
Refusing to even answer the yes/no 'Have you found such an exploit against the built-in MacBook card and driver?' is just silly.

Their ambiguity makes it every bit as likely that a malfeasant would investigate on his own than if they had came right out and said, 'We have identified a vulnerability in the default MacBook Wi-Fi driver and card.'
Why refuse to say 'yes' or 'no' but instead hint that there might be one?
Apple Legal can't simply impose a 'You won't talk about this exploit, nor will you even say that you're not allowed to talk about this exploit' gag order upon them.
And shouldn't a security research firm such as SecureWorks be prepared for bullying tactics from vendors?
In the meantime, the questions continue to come faster than the answers.
key_insight: The security researchers' refusal to give a straightforward yes-or-no answer about the MacBook's built-in Wi-Fi vulnerability is more damning than any answer they could give.
